sample_artists: And So I Watch You From Afar

eras.title: The Orchestra
eras.text: I love the sound of a whole orchestra and how they fill a room. Rimsky-Korsakov, Shostakovich — the old marches, the way everything builds. The drums, obviously. That fullness connects in a linear way to everything else I ended up loving.

albums.preview_track: Scheherazade, Op. 35 : Rimsky-Korsakov: Scheherazade, Op. 35: I. The Sea and Sinbad's Ship. Largo e maestoso
Symphony No. 5 in D Minor, Op. 47: I. Moderato

eras.title: On Wax
eras.text: Delta blues, race records, the original stuff on wax. Lightnin’ Hopkins, Robert Johnson, Charley Patton. I came to this much later in life — something really elemental about this music that I had to grow into appreciating. For the first time you’re hearing recorded music, captured in a room. The raw simplicity is the whole point.

albums.album: King of the Delta Blues Singers

albums.preview_track: Down The Dirt Road Blues (2010 Remastered)

eras.title: Big Band
eras.text: The swing era. Nat King Cole, Duke Ellington, Count Basie — the sound of a full band locked in together. This is where the drums and the arrangements got sophisticated. I came to love this later too. It all leads straight into hard bop.

albums.album: The Best of the Nat King Cole Trio

eras.title: The First Generation
eras.text: The first generation of Black artists who were massively influential. Sister Rosetta Tharpe was playing electric guitar before anyone. I came to appreciate this era much later, but everything starts here.

eras.title: Hard Bop and Beyond
eras.text: The psychedelic era, and the hard bop transition into jazz getting weirder. Jazz artists were more audacious than any other Black artists at that time — they had the persona, and the jazz club gave you a refuge in a different way. Sun Ra, Monk, Coltrane, Miles. I came to their work later in life and I buy and respect it so much.

albums.preview_track: A Love Supreme, Pt. I – Acknowledgement
So What (feat. John Coltrane, Cannonball Adderley & Bill Evans)

eras.title: Freedom Music
eras.text: Everybody makes a psychedelic record. There’s such an optimism to the freedom of that music, even during a really hard time in America. Gospel starts to get more contemporary, leaves the church, hits the streets in a different way. James Cleveland is the most influential for me in that era. My parents liked this stuff and I resisted it growing up — I came back to all of it much later and now I love it.

eras.title: New Tools
eras.text: Everybody’s experimenting. Prince’s debut, Anita Baker’s voice, Duran Duran and Madonna owning pop, Debbie Gibson going platinum at 17. Television and Depeche Mode rewiring what a band could sound like. Cocteau Twins making music that sounded like it came from somewhere else entirely. My parents liked a lot of this and I resisted it at the time — now I’m deep into all of it.

albums.album: Giving You The Best That I Got

eras.title: Jersey Club
eras.text: My parents would throw these huge parties. Everybody would come over — family and friends — and the music was great. I have memories of all that stuff. That sound was influential for my brain because I couldn’t avoid it, and now I’m nostalgic for it specifically.

eras.title: Finding My Own Voice
eras.text: I started buying my own music. The grunge and indie era was huge and I was actually listening to it — Pearl Jam, Soundgarden, Skunk Anansie. My tastes were already shifting to The Sundays, early indie pop like Broadcast and Black Tambourine, Tori Amos. Nas’s first album was monumental. The Roots became huge for me. Live’s Throwing Copper is still one of the most meaningful records to me. Counting Crows. This was the era of finding your own voice.

eras.title: The Scrobbling Era
eras.text: The most satisfying part of my modern listening life is how many people I get to share music with as a fellow fan. Years of sharing on Instagram stories, making friends on Twitter and social media just from our shared love of a few bands — maybe sharing the same industry and also liking the same artists. Moving to Portland really amplified this. So many little venues, so many artists at different parts of their ascent. This opened up a whole new world across genres. As a news anchor at KBOO FM I get to spend part of the summer anchoring the Waterfront Blues Festival and the Cathedral Park Jazz Festival live on the air. In 2025 I started my first radio show since college at Freeform Portland 90.3 FM — Sound Bulletin broadcasts every other week and is also available on Mixcloud.
